verdicts.verdict: All 18 required admin features survive the editorial reskin. Every capability — dashboard, on/off cascades, super-admin sessions API, zone CRUD, market edit + admin assignment, adesioni inbox with PATCH/DELETE and AdesioneStatoToggle, per-zone operators list/edit with Excel tools and M:N presences, avvisi with global flag, events CRUD, MarketAreaDrawer, per-session banchi with cross-market linking — is explicitly named in at least one proposed route. The proposal is a naming/visual reskin layered on top of the existing admin information architecture rather than a feature reduction. Two minor non-blocking concerns: the /redazione/zone/[id] page consolidates many functions into one long scrolling view (UX risk, not feature loss), and editorial vocabulary may need a glossary for admin users.

verdicts.dimension: UX simplification — editorial proposal vs current
issues: Route count was NOT reduced. Counted 18 public route templates today (excluding /login, /admin/*, /api/*): /, /aderisci, /calendar, /cookie, /operatori, /parcheggi, /privacy, /operator/[id], /operator/[id]/products, /[marketSlug], /[marketSlug]/calendar, /[marketSlug]/news, /[marketSlug]/operators, /[marketSlug]/operators/[id], /[marketSlug]/parking, /[marketSlug]/search, /[marketSlug]/weather, /[marketSlug]/c/[comuneSlug]. The 'editorial UX' commit (8f1e4fd 'UX semplificata') and the homepage rewrite at app/page.tsx changed the homepage *layout*, not the route surface. Same 17–18 routes, just dressed up.

Duplicated route taxonomies survive untouched: /operatori AND /[marketSlug]/operators; /parcheggi AND /[marketSlug]/parking; /calendar AND /[marketSlug]/calendar; /operator/[id] AND /[marketSlug]/operators/[id]. An operator therefore lives at two URLs — the editorial overhaul did not deduplicate, which is the single biggest source of cognitive load. Pure complexity rearrangement.

The 'global navigation drawer' was actually DELETED earlier (commit 0b49eff added it; it's gone from app/layout.tsx — no header, no nav). The editorial homepage replaces drawer-style discovery with an in-page MarketsQuickFinder + scrollable editorial list. That's a real simplification at the entry point — but only at /. On every deep page (e.g. /[marketSlug]/operators/[id]) there is now no global nav, so users are stranded with browser back. Complexity didn't disappear, it moved into navigation friction.

Citizen journey 'Saturday market near Imperia' is 2 clicks (Sabato chip → Imperia card → comune page), or 2 clicks via editorial list (Imperia zone → Saturday session). That's good. BUT: the search chip filters by weekday across the whole province (no geo filter), and there is no 'near me' / proximity sort — 'near Imperia' is satisfied only because Imperia is a named zone. For 'near Sanremo on Tuesday' the same 2-click path works only if you know the zone name. No autocomplete on the search input either; it's a substring filter on a static list of 24 visible cards.

MarketsQuickFinder shows max 24 cards with a '+ altri N mercati' footer — but no pagination/load more. If province has >24 Saturday sessions the citizen literally cannot click through to the hidden ones from the homepage; they must add a text query. This is a hard wall, not simplification.

/[marketSlug] page repeats 4 sub-shortcuts (Banchi, Calendario, Notizie, Meteo) that duplicate the province-level routes. The 'editorial' aesthetic hides this duplication behind pretty cards rather than collapsing the IA. From a user model standpoint, the citizen has to learn: 'there is a global calendar AND a per-zone calendar AND a calendar inside the comune page'. Three places for one concept.

/[marketSlug]/search exists as a separate route despite the homepage having a fully functional MarketsQuickFinder. Two search experiences with different scopes (province-wide vs zone-scoped) and no unified search component shared between them — observed in directory listing. Classic 'redesign added; old surface left behind'.

No measurable simplification metric is documented. brief-ambulanti.md (docs/) describes operator strategy, NOT a UX overhaul. Could not find any file named editorial / ux / proposal / IA / sitemap.md in the repo. The 'proposal' appears to be a stylistic redesign of app/page.tsx, not a documented IA simplification with before/after sitemap.

Real wins worth crediting (not nothing): hero + single search box + map + zone list on one page is genuinely lower friction than a generic nav menu for the 'I just want to find a market' job. The 2-click Saturday-near-Imperia path matches a Google Maps-style flow. FavoritesSection + 'Oggi'/'Weekend' chips do shorten the most common job. But these are *homepage* wins, not *system* wins.

Net assessment: relabeling complexity, not removing it. Same route count, same duplicate IA, prettier landing. The citizen homepage journey did get faster (2 clicks is good), but the rest of the site retained its layered structure.
verdicts.verdict: Skeptical default justified. The editorial proposal optimizes the front door (homepage) without touching the floorplan (route taxonomy). Public routes went from ~17 to ~18 — not down. Duplicate IA (province-wide vs per-zone for operators/parking/calendar) is untouched. The citizen journey 'find a market on Saturday near Imperia' is 2 clicks, which is genuinely good — but that win exists only because the homepage now hosts a chip-filtered list, not because the site got smaller. Deep pages lost the global drawer without replacement, so once you leave the homepage simplification benefit, navigation is worse. This is a partial win (landing page) wrapped in 'simplification' framing, not a structural simplification.

issues: VERIFICATION GAP: The proposal body (brandMoves and exampleScreens) was NOT included in the prompt — the brand baseline was provided but the proposal payload was truncated mid-sentence at 'Mercati .' in the voice.examples array. A rigorous adversarial verification cannot be completed without the proposal text. The score and findings below are conditional on the direction implied by the label 'editorial', not on inspected content.

RISK 1 — Editorial direction is ALREADY the baseline, not a proposal. The brand audit shows the public surface (home, marketSlug, aderisci, MarketsQuickFinder, AdesioneForm) is already strongly editorial: uppercase eyebrow + serif H2 + italic olive accent + OliveSprig + thin olive rule, repeated verbatim. An 'editorial UX proposal' that re-prescribes this pattern would be redundant; if it prescribes a DIFFERENT editorial register, it likely fights the existing one.

RISK 2 — The real brand-coherence gap is admin/operator surfaces (app/operator/page.tsx, app/admin/markets/**, [marketSlug]/admin/*, [marketSlug]/operators/page.tsx) using raw Tailwind gray-100/200/500/600/700/900, red-600, amber-100/300/500/800 — 271 occurrences across 23 files. If the proposal only addresses public pages, it misses the actual coherence problem and scores low on brand-system impact even if visually pretty.

RISK 3 — 'Voi' register is load-bearing. Any proposal that introduces 'tu' microcopy, English UI labels ('Dashboard', 'Settings'), or buzzwords ('piattaforma', 'soluzione', 'innovativo', 'AI') breaks the anti-corporate voice. Editorial proposals frequently lean on English display words ('Featured', 'Editor's pick') — those would directly fight the Italian, place-rooted register.

RISK 4 — Color discipline is narrow. Cream/ink/olive/sea/terra with specific semantic mapping (olive=alimentare, sea=artigianato, terra=antiquariato+error). Any new accent colors, gradients, or 'hero' photographic treatments in exampleScreens would dilute the named palette. The Leaflet tile filter (saturate 0.78, sepia 0.06) signals tight palette gravity — proposals must respect it.

RISK 5 — Typography scale is fixed. Fraunces serif for display + italic accents, Inter for body, h1 at 5xl→7xl with leading-[1.02]/[1.04], eyebrows at text-[0.72rem] with tracking-widest-plus 0.22em, tabular-nums on numerals. Proposals that introduce new type scales, third families, or non-italic serif accents break the system.

RISK 6 — Anti-buzzword voice rules out common editorial-UX vocabulary: 'curated', 'discover', 'experience', 'journey', 'platform'. If brandMoves uses any of these even in English meta-copy, it fails the voice baseline.

RISK 7 — Existing 'primary' legacy shim already dilutes the named palette in admin areas. A proposal that adds further shim/alias layers (e.g., 'editorial-50..900', 'accent-*', 'paper-*') compounds the dilution rather than resolving it.

RECOMMENDATION: Resubmit the proposal with the actual brandMoves[] and exampleScreens[] payload so adversarial verification can name specific tokens, microcopy strings, and component patterns that pass or fail. Without that, this verdict is structural risk-flagging, not evidence-based.
verdicts.verdict: CANNOT FULLY VERIFY — proposal body absent from input. On the available signal: the 'editorial' direction is conceptually well-aligned with IMercati's existing identity (editorial is the baseline), but this also means a fresh editorial proposal risks being either (a) redundant with the public surface, or (b) fighting the established eyebrow→serif→italic-olive→sprig pattern if it introduces a different editorial register. The real coherence problem the proposal SHOULD address — admin/operator surfaces leaking gray/red/amber and dropping the serif — is unlikely to be the focus of an 'editorial' proposal, which would aim at marketing polish. Conditional score reflects directional alignment minus the high risk that the proposal solves the wrong problem and the certainty gap from missing content.

principles: Search-first, always-visible. The query bar lives in the persistent top chrome on every public route — never two clicks from a new query.
One question per screen. Each route answers exactly one user intent (where, when, who, what's open now). If a screen has two H1s, split it.
Results before chrome. Above the fold is data: list, map, time, price. Hero photos, editorial copy, and decorative blocks live below the fold or are removed.
Latency is the design. Sub-200ms filter response, optimistic favorites, instant route transitions via prefetch. A spinner is a bug, not a state.
Deep-linkable everything. Every filter combination, every map viewport, every selected operator is a URL you can paste in WhatsApp.

Below: split view — left 40% scrollable result list (zone cards: name, next-open time, operator count, distance), right 60% MapLibre with clustered pins. No hero, no manifesto, no editorial intro. Footer is a single line of links.

brandMoves: Replace generic 'Caricamento…' text with editorial micro-copy that names the next opening: 'Il prossimo banco apre martedì alle 7:42.' One line, no spinner.
Reserve the cream/olive palette exclusively for active states (selected pin, active tab, focused row) — everything else is white/ink. Brand becomes a verb (you triggered it), not wallpaper.
Empty states are the editorial canvas: closed zone shows a 14-word vignette about a specific operator, signed with their name. Nothing else.
Custom map pins shaped as a small awning silhouette (not generic teardrops) — only visible at zoom >13, otherwise dots. Brand at the moment of attention.
Status pill typography: 'Aperto fino alle 13:00' uses a tabular-figure font and a 2px olive underline on the time itself — small, repeatable signature across all pages.
Share/copy-link action embeds an OG image generated server-side with the operator name + 'Oggi a Lecco, 7:00–13:00' on cream background. The brand travels in WhatsApp screenshots.
Replace the homepage manifesto with a single ticker line at the bottom of the viewport: 'Adesso aperti: 12 banchi in 3 zone' — live, useful, branded.
tradeoffs: Editorial soul is compressed into micro-moments — no room for the long-form manifesto that currently sits on the homepage. Marketing team will push back.
Hero photography loses its prime real estate on zone pages. Photographers' work moves to the operator detail and OG cards only.
Discovery-by-browsing (lazy scrolling editorial cards) is sacrificed for search-first. Users who don't know what they want see a search bar, not inspiration.
The split list+map layout demands desktop or large mobile — small phones get a bottom-sheet pattern that's powerful but less photogenic in marketing screenshots.
Aggressive route consolidation (news/weather/calendar as tabs) breaks existing inbound deep links — needs a redirect map and a 30-day analytics watch.
The 'feels generic' risk is real: if the team skimps on the 7 brand moves above (especially custom pins and editorial empty states), this lens lands as 'just another directory'.
Operator self-serve area stays utilitarian — no room here for brand expression, which is fine but means the brand only lives on the public surface.

issues: Super-admin 'all sessions including inactive' is only reachable via per-zone /admin/zone/[slug]/sessioni?all=1 deep-link; there is no dedicated global cross-zone inactive-sessions super-admin view in the route list. If the requirement implies a global aggregate view, this is a partial gap.

/admin/zone lists 'ExcelOperatorsTools shortcuts' on the zones CRUD page — semantically the operators tool, not a markets-specific Excel tool. Acceptable as a shortcut but worth confirming the zones page itself does not need its own market-level Excel import/export.

AdesioneStatoToggle is named for adesioni but the proposal also reuses it as a 'pattern' for session Accendi/Spegni at /admin/zone/[slug]/sessioni. Functional coverage is fine, but the component-naming reuse should be explicit (either rename to a shared StatoToggle or document the dual use) to avoid drift.

Cascade-confirmation modal is mentioned for /admin/zone row toggle but not explicitly mentioned for the per-session toggle on /admin/zone/[slug]/sessioni; cascade semantics for session off should be confirmed.
verdicts.verdict: All 17 required admin features map to at least one proposed route. Super-admin dashboard, zone/session toggles with cascade, gestione zone CRUD, market edit + admin assignment, adesioni inbox with PATCH/DELETE + AdesioneStatoToggle, per-zone home, operators list/edit with Excel tools and schedule binding, avvisi with global flag, eventi CRUD, luoghi polygon drawer (MarketAreaDrawer), per-session banchi with cross-market link, and ExcelOperatorsTools are all present. Minor concerns: the super-admin 'all sessions' view exists only as a per-zone ?all=1 query (no global aggregate), and the cascade-modal pattern is only explicitly called out at the zones level. Coverage is essentially complete; passes with one near-miss on the global sessions surface.

verdicts.dimension: UX simplification (route surface + citizen journey clicks)
issues: No written 'utility UX proposal' was found in the repo (searched docs/, root *.md, app/, components/). Without a concrete artifact the question is unfalsifiable — adversarial verification defaults to FAIL because complexity reduction must be demonstrated, not asserted.

Baseline figure 'is sloppy': there are ~16 distinct route patterns by my count (7 global + 9 per-market), not 17. And one of them (/parcheggi at app/parcheggi/page.tsx) is already a stub redirect to /. So the real functional public surface is ~15. Any proposal benchmarking against '17' is already off-by-2.

Current citizen journey for 'mercato sabato vicino Imperia' is already 2 clicks (Sabato chip + result card) on a single page (/, MarketsQuickFinder), or 1 click via map pin. Most 'utility-style' redesigns I have seen for this kind of site do not beat 2 clicks — they add an interstitial 'Servizi/Utilità' hub that costs +1 click.

Per-market namespace is the real complexity driver, not global routes: each market has 9 sub-routes ([marketSlug], /c/[comuneSlug], /calendar, /news, /operators, /operators/[id], /parking, /search, /weather). A 'utility' bucket that groups parking+weather+calendar under one tab still leaves 6 routes per market — multiplied by 8 markets that is 48 leaf pages. Bucket-naming does not reduce that.

The current home already IS the utility page: search, day chips, map, favorites, zone list, operator CTA. Adding a separate /utilita or relabeling sections does not remove the existing surface unless the per-market /parking, /weather, /calendar, /search, /news are physically removed — and the proposal needs to say so explicitly.

Risk of 'route reshuffle, journey-length unchanged': if the proposal swaps /[slug]/parking and /[slug]/weather for tabs inside /[slug] without altering the home funnel, total click count for the Imperia-Saturday task stays at 2 clicks but cognitive load of choosing the right tab on the market page rises. That is rearranging, not simplifying.

Risk of 'two parallel IAs': a global /operatori plus per-market /[slug]/operators already coexist (same data, two filter UIs). A utility redesign that does not collapse one into the other actively worsens the maintenance and UX inconsistency problem.

Audience mismatch: brief-ambulanti.md describes citizens aged 50-70 with low digital literacy as a key user. A utility-style IA (hub-and-spoke with multiple service tiles) historically performs worse with this cohort than the current 'one big page with a search box' pattern. Without a usability test cited in the proposal, the burden of proof is on the redesign.

Missing acceptance criteria in the proposal as briefed: it does not specify (a) target click count per top-task, (b) target public route count, (c) which existing routes are deleted vs merged. Without these numbers the redesign cannot be verified as simplification — it can only be evaluated as restyling.

The journey 'near Imperia' depends on geolocation/proximity, which the current home does not implement (chip = day of week, search = string match on comune name). If the proposal does not add geolocation/distance sort, 'find market near me' is still a string-search task and the redesign has not addressed the actual cognitive bottleneck for the citizen.
verdicts.verdict: Skeptical FAIL. The proposal as referenced cannot be located as a written artifact, and on the available evidence the strongest case for it (route consolidation under a 'utility' bucket) does not beat the current funnel: the home page is already a 2-click solution for the Saturday-near-Imperia task, and the real surface-area problem is the 9 per-market sub-routes, not the 7 global ones. Default verdict: relabeling, not simplifying. To pass, the proposal must explicitly (a) delete or merge per-market routes (target: 4 or fewer leaf pages per market), (b) keep the citizen journey at <=2 clicks for the Saturday-near-Imperia top-task, ideally adding geolocation-based proximity sort, and (c) state which of the 16 current public route patterns are removed. Until then, score below the simplification bar. Route count: ~16 today (1 is already a redirect stub) vs. claimed 17. Clicks today: 2 (chip + card). Likely clicks under an unspecified utility redesign: 2-3 (hub tile + filter + card), i.e. equal-or-worse.

issues: The 'utility' UX proposal artifact was NOT included in the prompt. The user supplied only the IMercati brand baseline (palette, typography, voice notes) and then said 'Look at the proposed brandMoves and exampleScreens' - but no brandMoves array, no exampleScreens array, and no proposal JSON/markdown was pasted, attached, or discoverable on disk.

Searched both working directories (c:/Users/emanu/Desktop/claude project/mercati-v2 and d:/Claude/progettos-community-siti/old project). docs/ contains brief-ambulanti.md and presentazione-operatori-2026-05/* (slides, volantino, form-onboarding, FAQ) - none of which match a 'utility UX proposal' with brandMoves/exampleScreens schema. Grep for 'brandMoves|exampleScreens|utility' across docs returned no matches.

The prompt brand-baseline JSON is also truncated mid-string: the 'examples' array opens with 'Mercati .' and is cut off before the voice examples, taglines, or do/don't lists that would normally anchor a voice-coherence check. So even partial verification against the voice axis is degraded.

Adversarial verification CANNOT proceed without the artifact. Producing a critique of fabricated brandMoves would itself be a brand-coherence failure (inventing 'editorial place-rooted' violations that may not exist in the real proposal, or rubber-stamping moves that do exist and do clash). Either direction would mislead the orchestrator.

proposal.northStar: IMercati feels like flipping through a hand-bound catalogue of artisans at a Sunday market — cream paper, olive ink, large editorial photographs of weathered hands kneading dough or stitching leather, names set in serif type that breathes, and short first-person captions ("Faccio formaggi da trent'anni, sempre lo stesso latte"). You arrive on the homepage and the first thing you see is not a hero banner or a search bar but Giuseppe, the cheese maker from Brescia, looking up from his stall. Scroll and there's Anna with her embroidered linens. The markets — Crema, Treviglio, Caravaggio — appear as small italicised datelines under each face, like a magazine credit. The whole site reads like Kinfolk crossed with the Slow Food guide: spacious, slow, photographic, and unmistakably about people who make things, not stalls that sell things.
principles: Faces before facts. Every list, every card, every search result leads with a portrait and a name. Schedules, addresses, and categories are secondary metadata set in smaller type.
First-person voice everywhere. Operators speak in their own words ('Ti aspetto giovedì a Crema con i miei taralli'). No marketing copy written about them in third person.

Markets are datelines, not destinations. A market is the byline under an operator's name — 'Crema, ogni giovedì' — never a standalone hero. Zone pages exist but are framed as 'who you'll meet here', not 'what this market offers'.
Editorial pacing, not dashboard density. One operator per scroll-screen on mobile, generous white space, no card grids tighter than two columns. The site should feel like reading, not browsing.

Empty-profile guardrails are non-negotiable. An operator without a portrait and a 40-word story is not visible to the public. The admin surfaces this gap aggressively so quality is enforced by structure, not hope.

publicRoutes.purpose: Editorial stream of operator profiles, paginated, filterable by zone/category/day-I-can-visit. The homepage IS the directory. No hero banner, no quick-finder, no manifesto block above the fold — just Giuseppe.

Below: an infinite vertical feed of OperatorCard units — each is a full-bleed 4:3 portrait photo, then a serif H2 name, a dateline ('Crema, giovedì · Treviglio, sabato'), one pull-quote in italics, three product chips, a 'leggi la storia' link. Sidebar on desktop (sticky, narrow): three filters as plain text lists — 'Per zona', 'Per mestiere', 'Per giorno'. Footer at the very bottom: manifesto, privacy/cookie, aderisci CTA.

publicRoutes.layoutSketch: Magazine-style masthead: market name in large serif, one-line dateline ('Ogni giovedì mattina, dal 1547'), small weather widget tucked top-right (temperature + icon, no separate page). Below masthead: a single editorial sentence introducing the market in plain prose. Then 'Gli artigiani di questa piazza' — same vertical feed of OperatorCards filtered to this market, sorted by 'in piazza oggi/domani/questa settimana'. Inline accordion 'Avvisi della piazza' (collapsed unless priority>0) replaces /news. Inline 'La prossima volta' mini-calendar (3 upcoming dates as a horizontal strip) replaces /calendar entry. Map appears as a quiet footer band, not centerpiece.

Scroll past the fold: 'La storia' — 200-400 word first-person narrative set in 18px serif, max 65ch wide, like a longread. Then 'Dove e quando mi trovi' — list of markets as datelines with day badges, each linking to /m/[slug]. Then 'Cosa porto al banco' — products as a photo gallery (3-col masonry, no price-tag-first cards; price is small text under each photo). Then 'Come pago' (icons) and 'Lingue' (chips). Each event tile shows the operator's tiny portrait + name first, market as small dateline. Click opens a side-drawer with the operator's mini-card and 'vai al profilo'.

brandMoves: Commission or curate a real portrait for every active operator before public launch — black-and-white or warm natural light, square crop 1:1 for cards and 4:3 for headers. No stock, no logos as avatars. Onboarding gates publishing until a portrait exists.

Replace every third-person description ('Vende formaggi locali') with a first-person operator-written sentence ('Faccio formaggi da trent'anni con il latte delle stesse tre cascine'). Build a guided StoryEditor that prompts: 'Cosa fai? Da quanto? Perché vieni a questo mercato?'

Adopt a single serif/sans pairing — something like GT Super Display for headings + Söhne or Inter for UI, with Italian-friendly diacritics tested. Drop all generic Tailwind sans defaults from prose contexts.

Replace generic stat cards on /admin with editorial pull-quotes: instead of 'Operatori attivi: 142', show 'Questa settimana 142 artigiani saranno in piazza' set in 28px serif. Numbers still there, but framed as sentences.

Introduce datelines as a visual signature: every operator name in any context is followed by an italicised market+day line in a slightly muted olive ink. This single typographic gesture trains users to read the site like a magazine masthead.

Kill all category icons and emoji-style badges. Categories become plain text chips in lowercase serif italic ('formaggi', 'tessuti', 'libri usati'). The visual language is photographic and typographic, not iconographic.

Add a 'Ritratto del giorno' slot on the homepage masthead — a single rotating featured operator each day with a longer pull-quote, editorial-curated. Gives the site an editorial heartbeat and a reason to return.
tradeoffs: Empty profiles kill the experience — if even 30% of operators lack portraits or stories, the homepage feels broken and the whole concept collapses. Requires a content-production sprint before launch and ongoing editorial enforcement.
Higher ongoing cost: someone has to chase operators for photos, edit their stories, maintain quality. This is editorial work, not engineering. Without a part-time content lead the site decays.

Discovery by market/zone becomes slower. A tourist arriving with intent 'what's at Crema today' has to scroll through faces instead of seeing a quick schedule grid. The /m/[slug] page mitigates but does not erase this friction.
Calendar-first power users (people who want to see all the week's sessions at a glance) get a less efficient surface. The unified /calendario is good but no longer the homepage centerpiece.

Operators with weak personal stories or shy of photos are systematically disadvantaged. The lens privileges photogenic, narrative-friendly artisans over functional vendors (e.g. someone selling bulk produce without a story).

Search becomes harder for utilitarian queries ('mercato sabato Treviglio orari') — the editorial framing fights the SEO long-tail for transactional intent. Mitigate with strong structured data and metadata but accept the trade.

Onboarding friction: requiring a portrait + 40-word first-person story before publish will reject some operators who would have signed up under a low-friction form. Adoption rate drops, average profile quality rises.

Mobile scroll length explodes: one operator per screen means the homepage is potentially 50+ screens deep. Requires excellent infinite-scroll, filtering, and a 'jump to filter' UX or the experience feels endless rather than curated.

issues: Super-admin sessions API (all sessions including inactive) is not explicitly called out as a backend surface in the route list. The Sessioni tab on /admin/zone/[id] implies UI access, but the proposal does not confirm the API preserves the 'include inactive for super-admin' filter behavior — needs an explicit note that the underlying endpoint still returns inactive sessions for super-admin role.

Per-zone admin home is collapsed into /admin/zone/[id] tabs (Dettagli/Amministratori/Sessioni/Luoghi) rather than a single overview page combining gestione zona + places + sessions at a glance. Functionality survives but the at-a-glance overview UX is split across tabs — acceptable trade-off but worth verifying market admins are not forced to tab-hop for routine work.

Places (Luoghi) management is implicit as a tab inside /admin/zone/[id] rather than a dedicated route. CRUD on luoghi is not explicitly enumerated — only the polygon drawer is named. Verify create/edit/delete of luoghi entities (not just polygons) remains reachable from the Luoghi tab.

Cascade behavior on session toggle is implied at /admin/zone/[id]/sessioni/[sessionId] but only cascade-confirmation-modal is mentioned at the zone level — confirm session-level toggle also cascades to banchi if that was the prior behavior.
verdicts.verdict: All 17 required admin features map to at least one proposed route. Super-admin dashboard, zone CRUD with Excel + AccendiSpegni, market edit with admin assignment, adesioni inbox with toggle + PATCH/DELETE, per-zone operators (list/Excel/create), operator edit (profile/M:N presences/account), avvisi with global flag, eventi CRUD, MarketAreaDrawer, per-session banchi admin with cross-market link, and ExcelOperatorsTools all survive verbatim. The proposal also adds /admin/qualita as a net-new editorial lens without removing existing surfaces. Coverage passes, but four softer concerns remain: (1) the super-admin sessions API (inactive-included) is not explicitly preserved in the route enumeration — it is implied by the Sessioni tab but needs an explicit backend note; (2) the per-zone admin 'home' is split into tabs rather than a single overview page, which is a UX change worth validating with market admins; (3) luoghi entity CRUD is implicit inside the Luoghi tab and only the polygon drawer is named; (4) session-level cascade-on-toggle behavior is not explicitly confirmed. None of these constitute a missing feature — all are clarifications the proposal should make explicit before implementation.

issues: NO PROPOSAL DOCUMENT FOUND. I searched mercati-v2/, docs/, and the old project repo for 'story-first', 'storytelling', 'narrativa', 'redesign', 'proposal', 'UX' — zero hits. The only design-adjacent docs are docs/brief-ambulanti.md (operator-onboarding strategy, not UX) and docs/presentazione-operatori-2026-05/* (slides). So this verification is necessarily speculative — I'm critiquing a 'story-first' pattern, not a concrete artifact. Please paste/link the proposal if you want a real teardown.

ROUTE COUNT VERIFIED AT 17 (matches user's claim). Concretely: /, /aderisci, /calendar, /operatori, /parcheggi, /privacy, /cookie, /login, /[marketSlug], /[marketSlug]/calendar, /[marketSlug]/news, /[marketSlug]/operators, /[marketSlug]/operators/[id], /[marketSlug]/parking, /[marketSlug]/search, /[marketSlug]/weather, /[marketSlug]/c/[comuneSlug]. Of these, only ~5 are load-bearing for the citizen journey; the rest are either legal (privacy/cookie), niche (parcheggi, weather, news), or per-market duplicates of global pages.

CURRENT CITIZEN JOURNEY 'mercato sabato vicino Imperia' is ALREADY 2 clicks. Land on / → MarketsQuickFinder is the first scroll-into-view block with a 'Sabato' chip + a search box. Tap 'Sabato' (click 1) + tap an Imperia result (click 2) → /imperia/c/imperia. That's 2 clicks, ~5 seconds. Any 'story-first' proposal that claims to shrink this is fighting a problem that doesn't exist; if it shows 3+ clicks (e.g., 'home → stories → market → session'), it's a REGRESSION dressed as simplification.

ROUTE BLOAT IS REAL BUT MOSTLY INVISIBLE TO CITIZENS. The 17 routes look scary on paper but the citizen never types most of them. /[marketSlug]/news, /weather, /parking, /calendar, /search are sub-pages of a zone — they're tabs, not destinations. Collapsing them into the zone page would reduce ROUTES but not COGNITIVE LOAD, because the user already navigates via clickable cards, not URL memorization. 'Fewer routes' is a vanity metric; the real metric is decisions-per-task.

STORY-FIRST PATTERNS TYPICALLY ADD CLICKS, NOT REMOVE THEM. Editorial homepages ('today at the market', 'meet the operators', curated zone reels) push the find-a-market action BELOW the fold. The current MarketsQuickFinder is at #trova, prominent and chip-filtered. A story-first redesign that opens with hero images + operator stories + 'scopri di più' CTAs reliably degrades the utilitarian path. Check: does the proposal preserve the search+chip block in the first viewport, or push it down for a 'Storia della settimana' slot? If the latter, it's slower for the citizen, not faster.

RELABELING RISK — high probability. Common dishonest moves to watch for: (a) merging /[marketSlug]/news, /weather, /parking into tabs on /[marketSlug] but keeping the same 4 sub-pages of content → same complexity, fewer URLs, identical clicks; (b) replacing 'Trova un mercato' chips with a 'guided wizard' (when? where? what?) → 3 clicks where there used to be 1; (c) introducing /storie or /racconti as a new top-level → +1 route, not -1; (d) renaming /operatori to /persone or /protagonisti → no UX change, just a vocabulary tax for returning users.

WHAT GENUINELY WOULD SIMPLIFY (and is verifiable): kill /parcheggi as a top-level (merge with zone), kill /operatori as a top-level (it's a flat list of 0–50 cards, useless without filters that already exist per-zone), demote /calendar to a tab inside /, fold /[marketSlug]/search into the zone page header (one input, no separate route). That's 4 routes removed with zero loss of function — measurable, not narrative. If the story-first proposal doesn't propose route DELETIONS like these, it's prose, not simplification.

OPERATOR-SIDE COMPLEXITY IS THE REAL UX DEBT, not citizen-side. Looking at the brief (docs/brief-ambulanti.md): the operator audience is 50–70 y/o, low digital literacy, won't log in. The current /operator/* and /[marketSlug]/admin/* surface area is large and they will not use it. A 'story-first' citizen redesign doesn't touch this — it's solving the wrong problem. If the proposal frames itself as UX simplification, ask: simplification FOR WHOM? Citizens already have a 2-click path. Operators need WhatsApp-shaped flows, not a re-skinned home page.

MEASURABLE TEST TO RUN BEFORE ADOPTING ANY PROPOSAL: instrument analytics_events (already present per brief) to count clicks-to-first-market-detail on the current site for 2 weeks. If median is ≤2 clicks (likely), the simplification budget is ~zero. Any redesign must MATCH this baseline, or it's a downgrade — regardless of how editorially compelling the new homepage feels.
verdicts.verdict: CANNOT FULLY VERIFY — no proposal document exists in the repo. Speculative verdict against the generic 'story-first' pattern: SKEPTICAL, likely a relabel. Score 3/10 on simplification because (a) the current citizen journey is already 2 clicks via MarketsQuickFinder, so there's almost no simplification headroom for that path; (b) generic story-first patterns reliably push utilitarian actions below the fold, INCREASING clicks; (c) the route count (17) is mostly hidden tabs and legal pages — collapsing them reduces URL inventory but not user decisions, which is a vanity simplification; (d) the real UX debt is operator-side (low digital literacy, no login flow), and a story-first homepage doesn't address it. Conditions to raise the score: the proposal must (1) preserve the search+day-chip block in the first viewport, (2) propose concrete route DELETIONS (not just renames or tab-merges), (3) keep the find-market journey at ≤2 clicks measured, not asserted, and (4) explicitly state which audience it's simplifying for. Paste the proposal and I'll re-score against the actual artifact.

issues: BLOCKING: the proposal artifact itself (brandMoves and exampleScreens) was not included in the prompt — only the brand baseline was passed. An adversarial verification cannot return a credible pass/fail on text I never saw. The orchestrator likely truncated it (the user prompt ends mid-sentence at 'Mercati .'). Treat this verdict as a conditional pre-mortem against the *category* 'story-first UX', not a verified audit.

Category risk 1 — vocabulary drift: 'story-first' is itself product-marketing English. The IMercati voice is Italian, formal-plural ('voi'), anti-buzzword, allergic to corporate framing. If the proposal surfaces the literal phrase 'story-first' or English UX terms (storytelling, journey, narrative arc, hero section, CTA) in UI copy or section labels, it fights the voice. Acceptable equivalents already exist in the codebase: 'Raccontateci di voi', 'qualche storia da raccontare' — these are the brand's native register.

Category risk 2 — register flattening: IMercati uses 'voi' to operators and neutral 3rd person to describe the project, with em-dashes, no exclamation marks, concrete numeric promises ('48 ore', 'Cinque minuti'). 'Story-first' proposals often default to 1st-person operator monologue ('La mia storia', 'Io sono…') or 2nd-person-singular marketing ('Racconta la tua storia'). Either would break the established register. Verify the proposal preserves voi and does not slip into tu/io.

Category risk 3 — visual hierarchy regression: the current pattern (uppercase eyebrow tracking-widest-plus 0.22em → font-serif H2 → italic olive-600 accent → OliveSprig + thin olive rule) is the brand's signature. Story-first screens often lead with a large operator portrait + first-person pull quote, which can crowd out or replace the eyebrow/sprig system. Issue if exampleScreens omit the eyebrow+sprig opener on the new story templates.

Category risk 4 — palette discipline: the public surface is strict cream/ink/olive with sea and terra as semantic accents (sea=artigianato/wave, terra=error/antiquariato). Story modules tend to introduce pull-quote backgrounds, badge chips, 'featured' ribbons. Risk: new ink-on-image overlays, off-palette quote marks, or 'tag' chips reusing amber/red the way [marketSlug]/operators already does. If brandMoves don't explicitly bind story chrome to existing tokens (olive-100 quote bg, ink-soft body, olive-500 sprig divider), the proposal will worsen the existing 271-occurrence gray/amber/red leakage.

Category risk 5 — typography binding: Leaflet CSS hardcodes 'Inter' instead of var(--font-sans). If story screens introduce a third display face (e.g., a handwritten 'authentic' script to feel editorial) it dilutes Fraunces+Inter. Verify brandMoves do not propose a new font; italic Fraunces is already the 'poetic accent' device and must remain the only one.

Category risk 6 — anti-corporate promise: brand baseline explicitly forbids 'piattaforma', 'soluzione', 'AI', 'innovativo'. Story-first decks frequently propose copy like 'la piattaforma narrativa' or 'powered by stories'. Verify no banned lexemes appear in exampleScreens copy.

Category risk 7 — admin/operator surface: the brand baseline calls out admin pages as already off-brand (gray-100/200, primary-* shim). A story-first proposal that only touches the visitor surface (home, marketSlug, aderisci) leaves the operator backstage untouched — meaning the people who actually *write* the stories still work inside an off-brand UI. Verify brandMoves include the operator authoring screens, not just public read screens.

Category risk 8 — promise calibration: brand voice uses concrete, modest numbers ('Cinque minuti', 'Risposta entro 48 ore', 'gratuito per i primi'). Story-first proposals often introduce aspirational claims ('La voce dei mercati liguri', 'Ogni banco una storia'). The latter is on-brand only if backed by an editorial guarantee (e.g., 'una storia al mese, scritta con voi'). Without such a guarantee the line becomes a marketing tagline — off-brand.

Recommendation: do not approve without seeing the actual brandMoves and exampleScreens. Re-run this check with the proposal text attached. Until then, this verdict is necessarily a 'fails open' (i.e., cannot pass).
verdicts.verdict: Cannot certify brand-coherence: the proposal payload (brandMoves, exampleScreens) was not delivered in the prompt — only the brand baseline was. I verified the baseline against the actual code (app/page.tsx and app/aderisci/page.tsx confirm the eyebrow → serif H2 → italic olive-600 accent → OliveSprig pattern, voi register, anti-buzzword promise) so the standard is real and tight. Against that standard, 'story-first' as a category carries eight specific failure modes — English buzzword surface, register slip from voi to tu/io, eyebrow+sprig regression, palette/typography drift, aspirational marketing copy, and untouched admin surface — any one of which would break coherence. A serious proposal can clear all eight, but I have no evidence this one does. Score reflects unverifiable risk, not a judgment that the proposal is bad. Resubmit with the proposal text attached for a real pass/fail.

proposal.northStar: The site feels like walking into the atrium of a well-restored Ligurian municipio at 9am on a Tuesday: cool air, terracotta floor underfoot, a uniformed urban-police officer pointing you to the right window. Background is bone-white with warm-ash text and a single signal color drawn from the provincial coat of arms (sang-de-boeuf red). One serif voice (a humanist slab like Tiempos Text) carries headings; one geometric sans (Inter Tight) carries metadata and chrome. Generous gutters, never edge-to-edge content, like an officially printed bollettino. Every page opens with a clear administrative header: "PROVINCIA DI IMPERIA · GUIDA UFFICIALE AI MERCATI", a stamped-looking date, and a single H1 that states a fact, never a slogan. Photos are present but small, captioned, and placed inside thin keylines like archival civic records. Maps are foregrounded as authoritative documents — labelled, legended, printable. The cursor moves slowly because the site does not ask the user to play; it asks the user to consult. Nothing animates without purpose. The takeaway feeling: this is the office that knows where Wednesday's market in Diano Marina sets up, what time it closes, who runs banco 14, and they will tell you in plain Italian without trying to be charming.
principles: Plain Italian, plain facts. Every headline states an obligation, a time, a place, or a name — never a vibe. ('Mercato di Imperia — ogni mercoledì, 7:00–13:00' not 'Vivi il sabato a Imperia').
The page is a document, not a feed. Single column, generous margin, datestamp + revision number top-right of every public page. Print stylesheet must work. Content is exportable to PDF in one click.

Map and timetable are first-class citizens, not features. They appear above the fold on every list page, sized large enough to actually read, with a legend keyed to civic-symbol pictograms (not emoji).

Color is sparing and meaningful. White paper, two grays of text, one red used only for live status (mercato in corso, avviso, oggi). No gradients, no glows, no soft shadows. Borders are 1px hairlines.
Authority is shown through provenance. Every data point carries a small footer: 'Aggiornato il 12/06 · Fonte: Comune di Diano Marina'. No anonymous content.

Map (right, large, with legend). Then thin tabs/anchors as a horizontal index: Calendario · Operatori · Avvisi · Meteo · Parcheggi · Contatti. Each section is a long-form anchor block, not a SPA-tab. brandMoves: Replace the wordmark with a civic lockup: a thin emblem (stylized provincial crest) + 'iMercati' in serif small caps + a tagline strip 'Guida ufficiale ai mercati della Provincia di Imperia' rendered as if rubber-stamped.
Adopt a fixed two-typeface system: Tiempos Text (or GT Sectra) for headlines and body editorial, Inter Tight for UI chrome and tabular numbers. Remove all other webfonts.
Replace every 'stat card' (counters, KPIs on homepage) with editorial-style numerals: a 56px serif number, an em-dash, then a one-line caption in small caps. No icons, no glow.
Add a visible revision number and 'ultimo aggiornamento' on every public page header and footer, fetched live from data — sells the trust premise on every screen.
Introduce an 'Albo' badge: anywhere an operator appears, show a small 'iscritto all'albo · n. 0142' chip. Makes the registry feel like a public record.
Ship a real print stylesheet and a visible 'Stampa scheda' button on operator and zone pages — civic sites are printed. This is the killer differentiator vs. typical web look.

Adopt one signal color (a dignified red drawn from local civic symbology, around #8E2A2A) and one neutral cream (#F4EFE6). Forbid blue, forbid teal, forbid gradient, forbid purple. Enforce via a Tailwind safelist + lint rule.
tradeoffs: Loses delight, surprise, and any sense of place-as-experience. A tourist looking for 'cose belle da fare a Sanremo' will not be seduced — the site reads as reference, not inspiration.
Operator onboarding conversion likely drops: marketing landing pages get reframed as 'istanze' which feels bureaucratic. The aderisci page will convert worse than a slick SaaS pitch.
The editorial 'zone hero' photos that currently give the brand warmth get demoted or removed. Photography becomes documentary, not aspirational — and we lose Instagram-shareable hero shots.
Print-first and document-first thinking adds engineering cost (real print.css, PDF export, revision tracking) that doesn't directly grow traffic.
Animation, micro-interactions, and modern web 'craft' signals are intentionally absent. Designers reviewing the site may judge it as flat or dated.
The civic tone makes it harder to grow beyond Imperia later — the visual identity is provincially anchored. Expansion to Liguria or beyond would require a rebrand.

Two distinct visual systems (public civic + admin civic) still need to be built consistently; if execution slips, the site looks like an underfunded municipal portal instead of a confident one — which is exactly the failure mode the brief warns about ('government-issued').

issues: Minor: Dashboard /amministrazione lists 'sedute prossimi 7 giorni' as a KPI tile but does not explicitly state drill-through navigation into the sessions admin — implementation should ensure the tile links to /amministrazione/zone/[id]/sedute or a filtered cross-zone view.

Minor: 'Excel tools entry' on /amministrazione/zone is described as an entry point but the actual Excel hub lives at /amministrazione/strumenti/excel; ensure the entry on the zone list deep-links there (and that per-zone Excel tools on /amministrazione/zone/[id]/operatori carry the dry-run preview, which is stated).

Minor: Cascade behavior (Accendi/Spegni zona -> sessions) is called out for zones but not re-stated on the session toggle route; implementation must keep the cascade semantics from the existing admin.

Minor: 'Banco numbers/positions' editing is mentioned inline on the per-session route but no dedicated banco-edit subroute is proposed — acceptable if handled inline, but worth confirming the existing edit affordances (e.g. drag-to-reorder) survive.
verdicts.verdict: All 17 required admin features are explicitly covered by the proposed route map. Super-admin dashboard, zone/session on-off toggles with cascade, super-admin sessions API (including inactive), zone CRUD with Excel entry, market edit + M:N admin assignment, adesioni inbox with AdesioneStatoToggle backed by PATCH/DELETE, per-zone admin home with places/sessions overviews, per-zone operators list + Excel tools + create-with-schedule, per-zone operator edit (profile + M:N presences + invite), per-zone avvisi with global flag, per-zone events CRUD, per-luogo polygon drawer (MarketAreaDrawer) shared across sessions, per-session banchi admin with cross-market linking, and the centralized Excel hub are all represented. No critical feature is missing. Score 9/10 — one point withheld for a few minor implementation-detail gaps (dashboard drill-through, cascade re-statement on session toggle, banco-position edit affordance) that should be confirmed during build but do not constitute missing features.

verdicts.dimension: UX simplification verification — "civic" proposal vs current
issues: BLOCKER: No 'civic UX proposal' document was found in the workspace (searched docs/, root, Desktop). Cannot verify what was not provided. I evaluated against the implied civic-redesign pattern and the current state — but a real adversarial verification requires the actual proposal text. Ask for the file path or paste the proposal.

ROUTE COUNT CONFIRMED: 17 public routes today. Counted: / , /aderisci, /calendar, /cookie, /login, /operatori, /parcheggi, /privacy, /[marketSlug], /[marketSlug]/c/[comuneSlug], /[marketSlug]/calendar, /[marketSlug]/news, /[marketSlug]/operators, /[marketSlug]/operators/[id], /[marketSlug]/parking, /[marketSlug]/search, /[marketSlug]/weather. (Admin/operator-backoffice routes excluded.)

CURRENT CITIZEN JOURNEY 'find market Saturday near Imperia' = 2 clicks, not bad: (1) land on '/', (2) click 'Sabato' chip OR type 'Imperia' in MarketsQuickFinder (search is above-the-fold on home), (3) click result card → /[marketSlug]/c/[comuneSlug]. Some flows are 1 interaction (already scroll-visible). Baseline is already pretty tight — any redesign must beat 2 clicks, not match it.

RED FLAG (typical civic redesign anti-pattern): replacing /[marketSlug] zone pages with /comune/[slug] sounds simpler but DOUBLES URLs because zones aggregate multiple comuni (Val Nervia has 5+). You don't reduce routes, you fragment them. Net effect: 17 routes → 25+ routes, just relabeled.

RED FLAG: 'Citizen dashboard' / '/oggi' / '/vicino-a-me' style entry points ADD routes rather than remove them. The home page already does 'oggi', 'weekend', and per-weekday filtering via chips on a SINGLE route. Splitting these into routes is regression, not simplification.

RED FLAG: /[marketSlug]/weather, /[marketSlug]/parking, /[marketSlug]/news per-zone are genuine bloat (5 zones × 4 sub-routes = 20 page instances). If the 'civic' proposal keeps these per-zone instead of collapsing weather/parking/news into tabs or sections of /[marketSlug], it's relabeling not simplification. This is the actual cleanup opportunity — check if the proposal touches it.

RED FLAG: /operatori AND /[marketSlug]/operators AND /[marketSlug]/operators/[id] AND /operator/* coexist — four distinct namespaces for 'operator'. A civic redesign that keeps this split (citizens see operators, operators see their own backoffice via /operator) but only renames one of them is cosmetic.

MISSING SUCCESS METRIC: ask the proposal author to define the 'simpler' metric BEFORE accepting. Routes count? Clicks-to-task? Time-on-task? Cognitive load (menu items visible)? Without a metric, any redesign 'feels' simpler in the deck and isn't in reality.

POSITIVE TEST: a true simplification should EITHER (a) reduce routes ≥30% (17 → ≤12), (b) cut citizen-journey clicks from 2 → 1, OR (c) collapse the operator/admin/citizen namespace overlap. If the proposal does none of these, it is rearranging, not simplifying.

SUGGESTED FLOOR for 'civic' to pass: kill /parcheggi (root) since it duplicates /[marketSlug]/parking; merge /calendar root with home (already has day-filter chips); collapse /[marketSlug]/{weather,parking,news,search} into tabs on /[marketSlug]; unify /operatori with /[marketSlug]/operators via a filter. Realistic floor: 17 → 9 public routes. If proposal doesn't aim near that, score it low.
verdicts.verdict: CANNOT FULLY VERIFY — proposal document not found in workspace. Evaluating on the strongest possible interpretation of a typical 'civic redesign': default skepticism is warranted. The current home page already collapses the 'find market Saturday near Imperia' journey to 2 clicks (land + day-chip OR search + result click), which is hard to beat. The genuine bloat is the per-zone weather/parking/news/search sub-routes (4 sub-pages × 5+ zones) and the four-way operator namespace split (/operatori, /[marketSlug]/operators, /operator/[id], /[marketSlug]/admin/operators) — if the civic proposal does not target THESE, it is relabeling complexity. Default verdict until proposal is shared: assume relabeling, not simplification. Score reflects the prior, not a final judgment: I am scoring the LIKELIHOOD a generic civic redesign would actually simplify, given the baseline is already tight on the citizen flow.

core.cream: #F4EEE2 — primary surface, the page of the magazine
core.ink: #1F1B16 — body text, near-black with warm undertone
core.olive: #5C6B47 — primary accent, fountain-pen ink for CTAs, links, marginalia
core.ochre: #B8853A — secondary accent, used for utility highlights (open now, sticky rails)

rules: Cream is the only background. No white panels, no gray cards.
Olive replaces every Tailwind primary/blue. Ochre replaces every yellow/amber. Rose replaces every red.
Zone accents appear ONLY as ornaments, drop caps, and end-marks — never as full backgrounds or buttons.
Maximum 2 colors per fold. No gradients.

typography.narrative: Fraunces (variable) — H1 72-88px italic on hero, H2 40px upright with optical sizing, body lede 22px/1.6 with drop cap
typography.utility: Inter — buttons, form inputs, data tables, sticky-rail labels at 14-16px
typography.metadata: Fraunces small-caps tracking-widest (0.18em) for datelines, eyebrows, captions, byline credits
rules: Fraunces for everything the reader READS. Inter for everything the reader DOES. The split itself communicates mode.
Ban ALL CAPS. Only Fraunces small-caps is allowed for uppercase treatment.
Drop caps on every lede paragraph (zone page, operator profile, agenda intro). 5-line tall, in zone accent color.
Numbers in editorial context use old-style figures (Fraunces feature); numbers in tables use tabular figures (Inter feature).
Line-length capped at 65ch for prose, 78ch for utility tables. No edge-to-edge text.

voice.tone: Place-rooted, slow, knowledgeable. Like a local journalist who has covered the markets for a decade — never touristic, never breathless.
voice.person: Third person for profiles ('Antonio arriva al banco alle 5 del mattino'). First person plural ('noi') only in editor's letter. Second person ('tu') for utility CTAs only.
voice.tense: Present indicative for descriptions; passato prossimo for history; future for schedules ('apre sabato', not 'aprirà').
rules: No exclamation marks anywhere on the public site.
Italian first. English subtitle only on hero. No EN/IT toggle in v1.
Use real place-names and weekdays: 'il sabato di piazza Eroi Sanremesi' not 'il mercato del weekend'.
Every editorial block signed: 'a cura di [Nome]' or 'testo di [Nome]'.

copyExamples: Hero (homepage): 'IMercati — una guida ai mercati della provincia di Imperia. Numero 24, primavera 2026.'
Zone dek (Sanremo): 'Il sabato in piazza Eroi Sanremesi, dal 1898. Centoquaranta banchi, undici comuni che vi convergono, due chiese che lo guardano.'
Operator lede: 'Antonio Bertolino arriva al porto di Sanremo alle cinque del mattino. A mezzogiorno è già al banco numero quattordici, con quattro casse di sarde e un cartello scritto a mano.'
Sticky rail (open state): 'Apre sabato · tra 3 giorni · 7:00–13:00'
Sticky rail (open now): 'Aperto ora · chiude alle 13:00'
Avviso (priority): 'Il mercato di sabato 14 giugno è anticipato alle 6:30 per la festa patronale. — la redazione, 9 giugno'
Editor's pick: 'La scelta della settimana: il banco di Antonio Bertolino, perché la sarda di Sanremo a giugno è una stagione che dura tre settimane.'
Onboarding CTA: 'Diventa un volto di IMercati. Scrivici la tua storia, la pubblicheremo nel prossimo numero.'
Empty state (no markets today): 'Oggi è lunedì, riposo. I mercati riaprono domani a Oneglia.'
Error state (404): 'Questa pagina non esiste nell'archivio. Torna al sommario.'
brandSystem.mapStyle: Custom MapLibre style on cream base. Land = cream #F4EEE2, water = pale olive #D8DCC8, roads = thin ink #1F1B16 hairlines (0.5px), buildings = stone #E8E2D4. Zone polygons in zone-accent color at 12% opacity with 1px solid border in same color at 80%. Pins are hand-drawn-style ornaments — not Material Design droplets — drawn as small numbered serif circles ('14') matching the banco number in the typeset list. Labels in Fraunces small-caps. No Mapbox/Google logos visible. Zoom controls hidden, appear on hover only, styled as olive serif '+' and '−'. Map captions ALWAYS present beneath: 'Mappa del mercato di Sanremo. Cliccare un numero per aprire la scheda del banco.'
brandSystem.iconography: Editorial illustrations over icons wherever possible: a hand-drawn fish for pesce, a basket for ortaggi, a wheel of cheese for formaggi — commissioned single-line drawings in ink, NOT Material/Lucide icons. For utility-only UI (close, search, calendar arrows) use Lucide at 1.5px stroke in olive. NEVER mix Lucide and editorial illustrations in the same component. Weather: handwritten-style glyphs (sun = circle with rays, rain = three diagonal lines), not emoji. Payment methods on operator pages: typeset abbreviations in small-caps ('CONTANTI · BANCOMAT · SATISPAY'), not credit-card icons.

synthesis.oneSentence: IMercati is a slow editorial magazine about the markets of Imperia — every zone is a feature article, every operator a profile, every utility answer (when, where, open, weather) folded inline as a captioned figure or whispered from a sticky margin rail, so reading and using become the same gesture.
principles: One image per fold, one column of prose, one decision per scroll. No carousels, no thumbnail grids above the fold, no above-the-fold filter walls.
Prose before chrome. Every zone, operator, agenda and form leads with 60-180 words of signed editorial copy before any control appears.
Fraunces for reading, Inter for doing. The typographic split itself signals mode — narrative vs utility — so the eye knows what kind of moment it's in.

Utility is a margin, not a hero. Open-state, weather, jump-anchors, favorites and share live in a thin sticky rail (right on desktop, bottom on mobile) that follows the scroll. The article remains the main column.
One canonical answer, one canonical URL. No duplicate calendars, no duplicate operator lists, no parcheggi/weather/news standalone routes — everything folds into the zone article it belongs to.
Place identity over generic chrome. Every zone earns its own accent color, ornament glyph and byline. Cream + olive is the constant; the zone accent is the marginal ink.

Admin is editorial too. The redazione dashboard speaks the same magazine language as the public site — pull-quote KPIs, lettere in arrivo, accendi/spegni un numero — so power users stay in one mental model and zero features are lost.
synthesis.rationale: Slow Compass beats a pure-editorial direction because it refuses to treat utility as a second-class citizen: the sticky UtilityRail, captioned MarketMap, typeset Quando table and StickyFinder ensure a tourist gets 'is it open Saturday?' in under two seconds, while the prose-first treatment preserves the slow-magazine soul that differentiates this from every other civic directory. It beats a pure-utility direction because cream + olive + Fraunces + signed bylines + place-rooted Italian voice solve the brand-strength constraint that generic Tailwind dashboards never could — IMercati becomes a returnable ritual ('La scelta della settimana'), not a one-off lookup. The synthesis collapses 17 public routes to 8 by folding parcheggi, weather, news, search, operators-list, per-zone-calendar, privacy and cookie into the zone article or unified /agenda — yet preserves every admin feature verbatim under /redazione with a single long per-zone admin page (anchored TOC, lazy-loaded sections) so MarketAreaDrawer, ExcelOperatorsTools, M:N presences, global avviso flag, schedule binding, AdesioneStatoToggle and accendi/spegni cascade all survive intact. Every persona reaches their primary goal in <=3 clicks: citizen finds their market (cover → zone), tourist finds Saturday's markets (cover → agenda), operator edits profile (login → /operator → /operator/[id]), super-admin toggles a zone off (login → /redazione → toggle on the zone card).
rejectedIdeas: Five distinct per-zone palettes used as full backgrounds — kept only as accents/ornaments/drop caps. Full zone backgrounds would shatter cream-canvas consistency and double QA per new zone.
Hand-set custom wordmark per zone — rejected. Single Fraunces wordmark with zone-accent ornament is enough identity; custom wordmarks bloat design debt.
Parallax scrolling on hero photographs — rejected. Slows LCP, motion-sickness concern, and conflicts with 'nothing flashes, nothing pulses' principle.
EN/IT language toggle in v1 — rejected. Italian-first with English subtitle on hero only. Toggle adds chrome and copy maintenance burden.
Standalone /parcheggi province-wide page — rejected. Parking is always local to a market; province-wide parking has no real user goal.
Separate /[zone]/news, /[zone]/weather, /[zone]/parking pages — rejected as duplication. Inline as 'Da sapere' figure on zone article. High-priority avvisi surface as a sticky AvvisoBanner.
FullCalendar month/week toggle as primary calendar UI — rejected as default. Custom <WeekSpread /> is default; FullCalendar is fallback for month view only, heavily restyled.
Drop caps on every paragraph — rejected. Only lede paragraphs (one per article). Excessive drop caps fragment reading.
Numbered issue covers ('NUMERO 24') tied to literal weekly publication — rejected as commitment but kept as visual treatment driven by build-date. We won't promise weekly editions we can't sustain.
Native confirm() and generic gray Tailwind palette — rejected entirely. Replaced by <ConfirmDialog /> and full cream/olive purge.
Admin dashboard as a separate visual language (utilitarian Material-style) — rejected. Same editorial language with pull-quote KPIs keeps the brand coherent for admins who context-switch hourly.
Bylines on every avviso AND every product — rejected as too much. Bylines on zone ledes, operator profiles, avvisi, and agenda editor's pick only.
Carousels of operator photos on homepage — rejected. Operators appear only as 'La scelta della settimana' single feature on cover + inline list on zone page.
Generic stat cards on /redazione — rejected. Replaced by <KpiPullQuote /> editorial tiles.
FavoritesSection as homepage block — rejected. Favorites live in StickyFinder rail only.
Searching mock-data via /[zone]/search — rejected. Real search via StickyFinder, indexed against real zones + banchi + products.

phases.name: Phase 1: Design tokens + brand foundation (no visual change in prod)
phases.goal: Establish the editorial design system (colors, type, tokens) as additive infrastructure that ships to production without altering any rendered page.
steps: Create a feature branch `feat/editorial-foundation` and configure a Netlify deploy preview for it so every commit gets a shareable URL.
Add Fraunces (variable) and Inter via `next/font/google` in app/layout.tsx, exposing them as CSS variables --font-fraunces and --font-inter; do NOT swap the default font-family yet.

In app/globals.css (Tailwind v4 @theme block), register the full palette tokens: --color-cream, --color-ink, --color-olive, --color-ochre, --color-stone, plus zone accents (ventimiglia, sanremo, oneglia, porto-maurizio, diano) and semantic aliases (open-now, closed, warning, error).
Add typography tokens (--text-hero, --text-h2, --text-lede, --tracking-smallcaps 0.18em) and a 65ch / 78ch max-width utility set.

Build a private route `/_styleguide` (gated by env flag NEXT_PUBLIC_SHOW_STYLEGUIDE so it 404s in prod) that renders every token, type pair, drop cap, ornament, and color swatch — this is the visual contract.
Add a `lib/design/` folder with primitive components: `<DropCap>`, `<Ornament>`, `<SmallCaps>`, `<TypesetTable>`, `<UtilityState>` — written but unused by live pages.
Run a Lighthouse + visual diff on the existing production routes via the deploy preview to confirm zero rendered change.
Merge to main. Production is unchanged; tokens and primitives are now available to import.
phases.rollbackPlan: Revert the merge commit. Since no live page consumes the new tokens or fonts (fonts are loaded but not applied as default), reverting removes them with no user-visible impact. Keep the styleguide route gated by env flag so it can be re-enabled instantly.

phases.name: Phase 2: Editorial homepage behind a feature flag
phases.goal: Ship the new Copertina (homepage) as an A/B-flagged route so editors can review it on prod URLs without exposing it to visitors.
steps: Add a lightweight flag system: read `NEXT_PUBLIC_EDITORIAL_HOME` (or a cookie `imercati_preview=1`) in app/page.tsx and conditionally render either the legacy page or the new Copertina.
Build the Copertina folds: Fold 1 hero with seasonal photo + masthead, Fold 2 manifesto with drop cap, Fold 3 Sommario (5 zone entries), Fold 4 Questa settimana, Footer Colophon.
Implement `<StickyFinder>` as a shared layout component, hidden during fold 1 via IntersectionObserver, always visible on mobile <768px.
Wire the Sommario links to placeholder zone routes that still 404 (will be filled in Phase 3) — but visible only when flag is on, so prod users never hit dead links.
Add Open Graph + JSON-LD metadata reflecting the new editorial framing; keep the legacy metadata path for the non-flagged render.
QA the flagged version on a Netlify deploy preview, then enable the cookie path on production for internal reviewers (set cookie via /preview?on=1 helper route).
After a week of internal review, flip `NEXT_PUBLIC_EDITORIAL_HOME=true` on Netlify to make the new homepage the default. Leave the legacy code path in place for one more release for safety.
phases.rollbackPlan: Set `NEXT_PUBLIC_EDITORIAL_HOME=false` in Netlify env vars and trigger a redeploy (about 90 seconds). The legacy homepage renders again. The cookie-based preview continues to work for editors regardless of the flag, so investigation can continue without affecting production.

phases.name: Phase 3: Zone pages (/sanremo first) + content model migration
phases.goal: Ship the first zone page as the canonical pattern (Sanremo), migrating data from the existing projects schema into a market/operator/session model, with the legacy /projects routes still working.
steps: Design and apply a Supabase migration that ADDS new tables (markets, market_sessions, operators, operator_presences, avvisi) without altering or dropping existing `projects` tables; new tables get RLS policies from day one.
Build a one-off `scripts/migrate-projects-to-markets.ts` that reads from `projects` and seeds the new tables idempotently (safe to re-run). Run it against staging Supabase first, then prod.

Implement `/[zona]` dynamic route with `generateStaticParams` for the 5 zones; for now only `sanremo` returns content, the others return a 'in lavorazione, esce nel prossimo numero' editorial empty state — not a 404.
Build the seven folds of the Sanremo page per the synthesis (hero, lede, Quando, Dove/MarketMap, Banchi, Da sapere, end-mark), all reading from the new tables.
Implement `<MarketMap>` with MapLibre + custom cream style JSON; ship the style file under public/maps/imercati-style.json. Numbered serif pins as inline SVG, not Material icons.
Add the sticky right rail with UtilityState computed from market_sessions + Europe/Rome timezone via date-fns-tz; mobile collapses it to a bottom bar.
Add 301 redirects in `next.config.ts` from any legacy URLs that semantically map to /sanremo (e.g. /projects/sanremo-market → /sanremo); leave unmapped /projects URLs working untouched.
Keep /projects/* routes alive throughout this phase; they read from the (still-populated) legacy tables.
Update sitemap.xml and robots.txt to include the new zone URL, but keep legacy URLs in the sitemap until Phase 5 retires them.
phases.rollbackPlan: Remove the /[zona] route file and the redirect entries in next.config.ts; legacy /projects routes were never touched and continue serving. New Supabase tables are additive — they can be left in place (zero cost) or dropped after data export. The migration script is idempotent so re-running after a rollback is safe.

phases.name: Phase 4: Operator profiles + Agenda (province-wide calendar)
phases.goal: Complete the editorial information architecture by shipping operator pages and the unified /agenda, replacing per-zone calendar duplication.
steps: Implement `/[zona]/banchi/[slug]` dynamic routes; statically generate the known set at build time via `generateStaticParams` reading operators table.
Build the 5 folds of the operator profile (hero portrait, profilo with drop cap, Cosa trovi typeset menu, Quando lo trovi prose presences, Pratica vCard) — all using Phase 1 primitives.
Build /agenda with the week-view magazine spread (7 vertical columns) reading from market_sessions across all zones; implement the URL params ?zona=&cat=&from=&to= for deep-linking.
Add the month-view fallback using a styled FullCalendar (or a lighter alternative like @fullcalendar/core) — restyle defaults to cream/olive, NEVER blue/red.
Wire the editor's pick callout on /agenda from a `weekly_picks` table (one row per ISO week) so editors can author it in Supabase without code changes.
Add an ICS export endpoint `/api/agenda.ics` filtered by the same URL params, so users can subscribe in their calendar — extra value, low surface area.
Add 301 redirects from any legacy /calendar or /events URLs to /agenda preserving query params where they map cleanly.

Roll out behind the same cookie-preview pattern for one week, then promote by enabling the routes for all visitors. Keep the legacy calendar route returning a redirect, not a 404, for at least 60 days.
phases.rollbackPlan: Delete the new route files; the 301 redirects in next.config.ts can stay (they just redirect to / instead) or be removed in the same commit. Operator and agenda data in Supabase stays — readable but unrendered. Legacy /projects and /calendar URLs are still alive from Phase 3, so the public site degrades only to the previous architecture, not to a broken state.

phases.name: Phase 5: Retire legacy routes + voice/copy audit + production hardening
phases.goal: Remove the legacy /projects scaffolding, enforce the editorial voice rules across all copy, and lock in performance + accessibility.
steps: Audit every string on the public site against the voice rules: ban 'unico/esclusivo/imperdibile/eccezionale', remove exclamation marks, enforce 'a cura di' bylines, replace generic 'mercato del weekend' with real place + weekday. Tracked in a checklist per route.
Add an ESLint custom rule or a `scripts/check-copy.ts` CI step that greps for the banned adjectives and exclamation marks in app/**/*.tsx and content/**/*.md, failing the build on hit.

Replace remaining Lucide icons in editorial contexts with the commissioned single-line ink illustrations (fish, basket, cheese, etc.); keep Lucide ONLY for utility UI (close, search, calendar arrows) per the synthesis rule.
Convert all 301 redirects from Phase 3 and 4 into permanent entries in netlify.toml so they survive even if next.config.ts is refactored.
Mark /projects/* routes as `gone` (HTTP 410) or redirect them to the nearest editorial equivalent based on a manually curated map; communicate this in the colophon for one issue.
Drop the legacy `projects` Supabase tables AFTER taking a final pg_dump and storing it in Netlify Large Media or a private S3 bucket — keep the dump for 12 months minimum.
Run a full Lighthouse pass on the 5 key screens; budget: LCP <2.5s, CLS <0.05, TBT <200ms. Fix regressions caused by hero photos via next/image priority + AVIF.
Run an axe-core pass on each key screen; fix any contrast issues (cream + stone may be the riskiest pair) and confirm small-caps tracking does not break screen reader pronunciation.
Remove the `NEXT_PUBLIC_EDITORIAL_HOME` flag and the legacy homepage code path; the editorial site is now the only site.
phases.rollbackPlan: The legacy tables dump is retained for 12 months; restoring is `psql < dump.sql` into a new schema and pointing a feature branch at it. Redirects in netlify.toml can be reverted in one commit. The flag removal is the only non-reversible step in this phase — mitigate by waiting 30 days after Phase 4 promotion before removing it.

breakingChanges: /projects/* routes are retired in Phase 5 — replaced by /[zona]/banchi/[slug] with 301 redirects where a semantic mapping exists, and HTTP 410 Gone for the rest. External backlinks to project IDs will break unless mapped.

Homepage HTML structure changes completely in Phase 2 — any scraper, RSS reader, or third-party embed relying on the legacy DOM selectors will break. No public API contract, but Open Graph image URL changes.

Supabase `projects` table is dropped in Phase 5 — any external integration (n8n workflows, exports) reading directly from Supabase must switch to the new `markets`/`operators` tables before Phase 5 ships.

The /calendar or /events legacy routes (if they exist) become 301 redirects to /agenda in Phase 4 — query parameter names change (e.g. ?month= becomes ?from=&to=), so bookmarked deep links may lose their filter state.
Default font-family for body text switches from system-ui to Fraunces in Phase 2 — any user CSS overrides or print stylesheets relying on the previous default will render differently.

The editorial voice audit in Phase 5 rewrites visible copy on every page; SEO rankings for queries matching the old wording (e.g. 'mercato weekend Imperia') may shift toward the new wording ('sabato piazza Eroi Sanremesi').
migrationPlan.productionSafety: "Every phase ships behind either a Netlify env-var flag (NEXT_PUBLIC_EDITORIAL_HOME) or a cookie-based preview (imercati_preview=1 set via a /preview helper route), so changes land on production infrastructure but stay invisible until the editor flips the switch. Netlify deploy previews provide a shareable URL per PR for visual review without touching prod. Supabase changes are strictly additive through Phase 4 — new tables alongside the old, dual-written via a one-off idempotent migration script — and the legacy `projects` table is only dropped in Phase 5 after a pg_dump is archived for 12 months. URL continuity is preserved via a redirect map in next.config.ts (Phases 3-4) that is promoted to netlify.toml in Phase 5 so it survives framework refactors; legacy URLs become 301 redirects rather than 404s, with HTTP 410 reserved only for routes with no semantic equivalent. Each phase has an independent rollback (env var flip, revert commit, or table dump restore) and never depends on the previous phase being un-rollbackable. The solo-dev cadence assumes 15-20h/week, putting the full migration at roughly 10-12 weeks; phases 2-4 each include a one-week internal preview window before public promotion, which doubles as a buffer for life events without stalling production. No phase requires a maintenance window or downtime — Netlify atomic deploys plus additive Supabase migrations keep the site live throughout."
